title: Peach Cobbler with Tinned Peaches

overview.description: A warm peach cobbler made with tinned peaches and topped with soft, golden sweet biscuit dough.

method: Drain the peaches, reserving 150ml of the syrup.
Place the peaches in a large baking dish.
Mix the reserved syrup with the brown sugar, cornflour, vanilla, cinnamon, nutmeg and salt.
Pour the mixture over the peaches.
For the topping, mix the flour, brown sugar, baking powder and salt in a bowl.
Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs.
Add the cream and vanilla extract.
Mix gently until a soft dough forms.
Drop spoonfuls of the dough over the peaches, leaving some gaps between them.
Bake for 35–45 minutes until the peaches are bubbling and the topping is golden brown and cooked through.
Allow to rest for 10 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: American cobbler toppings vary considerably by region, ranging from biscuit-like dough to cake batter and pastry-style toppings.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to turn tinned fruit into a balanced baked fruit pudding and how to make a soft biscuit-style cobbler topping.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: An inexpensive, reliable fruit pudding using tinned peaches from the pantry rather than expensive fresh fruit.
chapman-tips: Choose peaches in juice rather than heavy syrup if possible.
Don't discard all the syrup; it helps create the sauce.
If using peaches in heavy syrup, reduce or omit the added sugar.
Leave gaps between the topping pieces so the peach filling can bubble through.
Don't overwork the topping dough or it may become tough.
The cobbler thickens slightly as it cools.
Tinned peaches provide consistent results regardless of season.
A little almond extract can be used instead of some of the vanilla for a classic peach-and-almond flavour.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The topping is at its best on the day it is baked.
notes.leftovers: Good reheated gently, although the topping will soften.
